sourceMovie.title: Nanny McPhee
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Nanny McPhee works as a fantasy movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The fantasy and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Onward
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Onward draws from the same fantasy well as Nanny McPhee, channeling comedy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2020 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Mary Poppins
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Mary Poppins (1964) captures the fantasy appeal of Nanny McPhee without simply imitating it. The comedy streak running through both films is what puts Mary Poppins at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Matilda
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy appeal of Nanny McPhee — that funny edge, the comedy — translates cleanly into Matilda (1996). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Mary Poppins Returns
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Mary Poppins Returns (2018) clearly understood what makes fantasy work — the same quality that keeps Nanny McPhee rewatchable. Expect the same funny rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Nanny McPhee and the Big Bang
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Nanny McPhee and the Big Bang (2010) is the kind of film Nanny McPhee fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The fantasy overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Ice Age
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Ice Age shares Nanny McPhee's gift for comedy — the 2002 film delivers family without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Nanny McPhee compelling — the fantasy, the family — shows up in The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe (2005) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that uplifting storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Cinderella
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy energy of Nanny McPhee is alive in Cinderella (1950), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: The Little Prince
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Little Prince (2015) operates in the same fantasy as Nanny McPhee, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The uplifting payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Practical Magic
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Practical Magic (1998) belongs to the same conversation as Nanny McPhee — both anchor their fantasy in comedy emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

faq.question: What should I watch after Nanny McPhee?
faq.answer: Try Onward, Mary Poppins, Matilda—each shares DNA with Nanny McPhee while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Nanny McPhee?
faq.answer: Nanny McPhee (2005) is best described as Fantasy, Comedy, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Nanny McPhee part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Nanny McPhee Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
